# Welcome Comp 110 EX09
## Our Hypothisis 
Our project analyzes whether students with no prior coding experience benefit more from increased tutoring hours and improved tutoring effectiveness. We focus specifically on small group tutoring sessions to determine if these sessions help students gain a stronger understanding of course material and improve their overall academic performance.

This research is valuable because it helps identify whether current tutoring sessions are being used effectively or if they need improvement. By understanding how students engage with tutoring resources, we can determine if the sessions are beneficial or if adjustments should be made to better support student success.

# Our Findings 

## Visualizations of tutoring effectiveness
<img src="personal.site.github.io/static/imgs/graph1.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>
These graphs show the percentage and population of tutoring effectiveness with the students that had no experience overlaid. We did this to visualize what the students thought about how effective tutoring was and to see how many of those students had no experience. We plotted the percentages because we realized that without that visualization the total population of students will always be more than the students with no experience, yet it still gives us a good comparison of how many of the total students have no experience.
We learned that most of the people that answered the question thought tutoring was effective. With the answers ranging from 1-7 most of the answers fall in the 5-7 range. Additionally the students who have no experience were the majority of the responses in the 7 category. Telling us that our theory was correct, students with no previous coding experience find the tutoring effective.
## Understanding vs tutoring effectiveness
<! need graph 3 here>
This graph shows a heat map of students' responses to the questions about course understanding and the effectiveness of tutoring. The center of this map has the most responses with the rest of the data being skewed to the right. This suggests that students do not think that they understand the course material better if they think that tutoring is effective. This is not what we expected to find. A potential explanation could be that students did not take the survey seriously and were just clicking the middle answer for all of the questions, thus explaining why the (4,4) cube is the most populated.
## Conclusion  
We recommend that COMP 110 increases the amount of tutoring hours because the data shows that students with none to less than one month of coding experience believe that they are beneficial. As an introductory computer science course, the team behind COMP 110 should advocate for people with no coding experience the most. When considering the people who went to the tutoring sessions, the majority of the responses claimed that they thought the tutoring sessions were 5, 6 or 7 in effectiveness. Now, when considering people who have little to none coding experience and comparing it to the entire class's perspective on tutoring sessions, there is a slight increase in the percentage of non coding student's belief in tutoring efficiency. When looking at tutoring effective vs understanding in all students that attended tutoring sessions, there doesn't seem to be a correlation. Despite this, tutoring is still effective since the survey was based on what students themselves thought rather than a more accurate measure like quiz scores.
If we have more tutoring sessions available, then more students can participate and eventually do better in the class. While this would create more work for TAs, it would overall benefit the students. Despite a lack of a correlation of students who think tutoring is helpful and how well they think they understand the content, we should still increase the amount of tutoring hours based on the fact that a majority of the population believes they are beneficial.
